description: Cross-source discoveries mined or curated from scubaSeason reef health, pressure, sightings, encounters, and IUCN datasets. These are story-ready findings backed by explicit comparison signals.

originalityScale.meaning: The core finding is already widely described by scientists, operators, media, or destination guides. Use as context, not as a scubaSeason discovery.
The underlying fact is known, but scubaSeason independently ranked it against our own dataset.
The finding comes from joining two or more datasets that are usually discussed separately.
The comparison appears to be non-obvious publicly and should be reviewed before being promoted as an original scubaSeason finding.

discoveryTypes.label: Reef outperforming its neighborhood
discoveryTypes.meaning: A reef has a measured condition signal that is much stronger than its regional or peer baseline.

discoveryTypes.label: Rare encounter stronghold
discoveryTypes.meaning: A threatened, unusual, seasonal, or bucket-list species has unusually strong repeat evidence at a site or region.

discoveryTypes.label: Heat resilience anomaly
discoveryTypes.meaning: A reef has stayed comparatively intact despite thermal stress, bleaching history, or regional decline.

discoveryTypes.label: Fresh eyes needed
discoveryTypes.meaning: The data is interesting but uncertainty is high because the latest direct survey or post-disturbance evidence is incomplete.

records.title: Tela is the Caribbean reef that is still holding on

originality.scubaSeasonContribution: Useful as a flagship explanation of the product, but the core resilience story predates our analysis.

records.dek: Tela Bay has roughly 60 percent live coral cover after the 2023-2024 bleaching period, while the wider Caribbean/Mesoamerican baseline in the dataset is closer to the high teens or low twenties.
records.claim: Tela is not just marked 'thriving'; it is a regional outlier with unusually high coral cover, dense Diadema urchins, and documented heat-tolerant coral symbionts.
records.whyItMatters: This is the kind of reef divers can talk about as living research: scientists are trying to understand why it has resisted losses that damaged nearby Caribbean reefs.

comparison.baselineLabel: Caribbean/Mesoamerican regional average cited in reef-health notes
comparison.method: Compare Tela's observed coral cover against the regional average cited in its reef-health notes and against other Caribbean post-2023 records in reef-health.json.

reasonSignals.label: Diadema urchins are unusually dense
reasonSignals.value: 2.5 per m2 at Tela vs roughly 1 per 100 m2 Caribbean average

reasonSignals.label: Elkhorn corals host heat-tolerant symbionts
reasonSignals.value: University of Miami Baker lab sampling found clade D zooxanthellae in Tela elkhorn corals

reasonSignals.label: Formal protection is active
reasonSignals.value: AMATELA Marine Wildlife Refuge and Jeannette Kawas National Park are cited in pressure data; closed fishing seasons reportedly produced a roughly 500 percent fish biomass increase in coastal lagoons

records.uncertainty: The 60 percent figure is a conservative post-2024 estimate; the file notes that no peer-reviewed post-2024 survey figure was published as of June 2026.

logic: Find locations where observed.coralCoverPercent >= 2.5x an explicit regional baseline or peer average.
Require at least one explanatory reasonSignal from observed.notes, pressure.visitorImpactNote, or source-backed research notes.
Attach uncertainty when the latest survey is estimated, post-disturbance, or not peer-reviewed.

records.title: Wakatobi did not behave like a normal heat-stressed reef

originality.scubaSeasonContribution: We can use the atlas to place the heat-tolerance signal beside travel relevance, but the resilience mechanism comes from existing monitoring and publications.

records.dek: The reef-health notes say Wakatobi recorded no significant bleaching during the 2024 global bleaching event, even though 84 percent of the world's reefs experienced bleaching-level heat stress.
records.claim: Wakatobi is a heat-resilience anomaly: its coral cover is not the highest in the atlas, but its response to heat is unusually strong.
records.whyItMatters: This lets the site surface a more interesting discovery than a simple health badge: some reefs matter because they are high-cover, others because they appear unusually heat tolerant.

comparison.baselineValue: 84% of world reefs exposed to bleaching-level heat stress
comparison.baselineLabel: global 2024 bleaching context in reef-health notes
comparison.comparisonText: no significant bleaching while most world reefs experienced bleaching-level heat stress
comparison.method: Compare Wakatobi's thermalStress and observed notes against the global 2024 bleaching context stored in reef-health.json.

reasonSignals.label: Locally evolved heat tolerance is cited
reasonSignals.value: The reef-health notes state Wakatobi's 2024 non-bleaching was attributed to locally evolved heat tolerance

reasonSignals.label: Long monitoring record
reasonSignals.value: Operation Wallacea / LIPI monitoring is cited across 2006-2019, with site-level cover reaching 46 percent in newer survey notes

records.uncertainty: The park-wide coral cover value is from a 2019 survey; the 2024 resilience claim is stronger than the current cover number.

logic: Find records where thermalStress.alertLevel is no-stress or watch while notes cite a major regional/global bleaching event.
Promote only when notes include a mechanism or monitoring context, not just a low alert value.

records.title: Sogod Bay combines a whale shark stronghold with unusually healthy outer reefs

originality.scubaSeasonContribution: The whale shark and reef-health facts are public separately; the stronger product finding is the join between a repeat megafauna record and an above-baseline reef-health signal.

records.dek: The reef-health notes cite outer Panaon Island reefs at 60-70 percent live coral cover, about 3x the Philippine national average, plus 350+ individual whale sharks documented over more than a decade.
records.claim: This is not just a place where whale sharks show up; the same location has a reef-health outlier signal and a long-running individual-identification record.
records.whyItMatters: The comparison gives divers a stronger reason to care: the encounter is tied to a broader conservation and reef-health story, not only a bucket-list animal.

comparison.subjectValue: 60-70% outer reef cover; 350+ individual whale sharks
comparison.comparisonText: outer reefs roughly 3x the national coral-cover average, with a decade-scale whale shark photo-ID record
comparison.method: Read reef-health notes for explicit national-average comparison and encounter notes for documented individual-count evidence.

reasonSignals.label: Whale shark individual records are unusually deep
reasonSignals.value: LAMAVE has documented 350+ individual whale sharks over 10+ years

reasonSignals.label: Outer reefs are far above national average
reasonSignals.value: Oceana Philippines survey notes cite 60-70 percent live coral cover on outer Panaon Island reefs

records.uncertainty: The stored 45 percent coral-cover value is a conservative bay-wide midpoint; the strongest 60-70 percent signal applies to outer Panaon Island, not every site in Sogod Bay.

logic: Find threatened or bucket-list species with long-running individual-count or high repeated-sighting evidence.
Boost when the same location has a coral-cover comparison against a national or regional baseline.
Keep sub-area caveats when the strongest reef-health value applies to one part of the location.

records.title: Osprey Reef looks buffered from the inshore GBR story

originality.scubaSeasonContribution: Remote outer-shelf buffering is publicly discussed; our current version needs a numeric peer comparison before it can be promoted as an original finding.

records.dek: Osprey Reef's 2024 record shows 48 percent coral cover, 6 percent bleaching, and 1 percent mortality, with notes pointing to its remote Coral Sea position and cooler upwelling.
records.claim: This is a peer comparison discovery: the reef-health record explicitly contrasts Osprey's minor bleaching with inshore GBR reefs affected more strongly by the 2024 mass-bleaching event.
records.whyItMatters: It gives divers a concrete reason to understand place, not just condition: offshore geography can make the reef experience very different from better-known inshore reefs.

comparison.baselineValue: inshore GBR reefs affected more severely
comparison.comparisonText: minor bleaching in a year that hit inshore GBR reefs harder
comparison.method: Use Osprey's observed bleaching/mortality values and the explicit inshore-GBR contrast in reef-health notes.

reasonSignals.label: Remote offshore atoll
reasonSignals.value: The notes place Osprey roughly 350 km from the GBR, with minimal human pressure

reasonSignals.label: Cooler upwelling is cited
reasonSignals.value: Steep oceanic walls and cooler upwelling are listed as thermal-stress buffers

records.uncertainty: The inshore comparison is qualitative in the current data; a future mining pass should compare Osprey numerically against nearby GBR records.

logic: Find high-cover or low-bleaching reefs whose notes include explicit peer-region contrast.
Extract geographic or oceanographic explanation signals from notes.

records.title: Ningaloo has the strongest whale shark repeat-sighting signal in the current sightings file

originality.scubaSeasonContribution: The whale shark aggregation is famous; our only original contribution is ranking it first by recentRecordCount in the sightings file.

records.dek: The Ningaloo whale shark encounter record has 412 recent records, the highest recentRecordCount in sightings.json.
records.claim: This is a comparison mined directly from sightings: Ningaloo is not merely a famous whale shark destination; it currently has the top repeat-evidence count in the dataset.
records.whyItMatters: This gives the app a clean, data-native discovery hook: 'the strongest whale shark signal we found,' backed by record counts instead of reputation.

comparison.baselineLabel: 442 sighting records as of this data snapshot
comparison.comparisonText: highest recentRecordCount in the sightings dataset
comparison.method: Sort sightings.json by recentRecordCount descending and join siteId to sites.json to resolve locationId.

reasonSignals.label: The species is globally endangered
reasonSignals.value: Rhincodon typus is listed as Endangered with decreasing population trend in iucn-status.json

records.uncertainty: recentRecordCount is not a per-trip probability because the dataset does not include a complete effort denominator.

logic: Promote records where confidence is high and the species has IUCN category VU, EN, or CR.
Always label as repeat evidence, not a guaranteed encounter rate.

records.title: Malapascua's thresher shark signal is unusually strong for an endangered shark

originality.scubaSeasonContribution: Monad Shoal's thresher reliability is famous; our contribution is ranking it among threatened-shark repeat-sighting signals.

records.dek: Monad Shoal has 287 recent pelagic thresher shark records, one of the highest single-species encounter counts in the current sightings file.
records.claim: This is a rare-encounter comparison: a globally endangered shark has a high-confidence, high-repeat sighting cluster at one dive site.
records.whyItMatters: For divers, it turns 'you might see threshers' into a more informative claim: this is one of the strongest endangered-shark repeat signals in the atlas.

comparison.metric: sightings.recentRecordCount filtered by threatened shark species
comparison.baselineValue: other threatened shark sightings in sightings.json
comparison.baselineLabel: sightings joined to IUCN categories VU/EN/CR
comparison.comparisonText: one of the highest repeat counts for an endangered shark in the atlas
comparison.method: Join sightings speciesScientific to iucn-status.json, filter sharks with category EN/CR/VU, then rank by recentRecordCount.

reasonSignals.label: High repeat evidence at one named site
The species is globally endangered
reasonSignals.value: Alopias pelagicus is listed as Endangered with decreasing population trend in iucn-status.json

records.uncertainty: The surrounding reef-health record says the marquee thresher dive does not depend on coral, so this discovery should be rendered as an encounter stronghold, not a reef-health win.

logic: Join sightings to IUCN status and site/location records.
Rank threatened shark sightings by recentRecordCount.
If reef-health notes say the encounter is decoupled from coral condition, keep the discovery in encounter framing.
